title: Meta drops 5% after Zuckerberg says AI agents "haven't accelerated"
subtitle: A leaked town hall recording turns a $145B capex bet into the first major CEO walkback of the AI-acceleration thesis.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Meta shares closed down about 5 percent yesterday after Reuters published a recording of a July 2 internal town hall in which Mark Zuckerberg told employees that AI agent development "over at least the last four months hasn't really accelerated in the way that we expected." Zuckerberg also conceded that the reorganization built around that push had not been "clean" and that the new structure's bets "haven't come to fruition yet," though he told staff he expects meaningful returns within three to six months.

The admission lands against a specific bill of goods. Meta cut roughly 8,000 employees in May — about 10 percent of its 80,000-person workforce — reassigned 7,000 more into AI-focused teams, cancelled 6,000 planned hires, and committed to between $125 billion and $145 billion in 2026 capex, more than double last year's outlay. The May layoff memo declared AI "the most consequential technology of our lifetimes." Six weeks later the CEO is telling employees the trajectory did not match the memo.

This is the first time a Big Tech CEO has publicly conceded that agent progress is behind schedule, and markets treated it accordingly. It also matches the softer signal from July 4 — when Sam Altman and Dario Amodei both walked back their earlier AI jobs-apocalypse language — into a harder one: the industry's most aggressive spender is now telling its own workforce the payoff curve slipped. Meta earnings later this month will be the next real test of how much slack investors are willing to give.

For learners and early-career engineers: the news is not that AI stopped working. It is that timelines for autonomous agents doing useful multi-step work at scale are harder than the most aggressive 2025 pitches implied. That gap is where the next few years of jobs sit — building the scaffolding, evaluation, and human-in-the-loop plumbing that turn capable models into reliable systems.

sources.title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven't progressed as quickly as he'd hoped — TechCrunch
Mark Zuckerberg Admitted AI Agents "Hasn't Really Accelerated" as Meta Stock Dropped 5% — The Motley Fool
Meta Stock Drops. Zuckerberg Has a Warning About AI Agents — Yahoo Finance
